subject: Surgery
stem: A 28-year-old woman presents with a painless thyroid nodule. Fine needle aspiration cytology shows cells with ground-glass nuclei (Orphan Annie eye nuclei), nuclear grooves, and intranuclear cytoplasmic inclusions. Histology also reveals concentrically calcified structures. Which of the following best describes these calcified structures?
options: Schaumann bodies
Psammoma bodies
Asteroid bodies
Calcium oxalate crystals
explanation: Psammoma bodies are laminated, concentrically calcified structures seen in papillary thyroid carcinoma (PTC). They represent calcified necrotic tumor cells and are a hallmark of PTC. The Orphan Annie eye nuclei (ground-glass, empty-appearing nuclei) along with nuclear grooves and intranuclear pseudoinclusions are the defining cytological features of PTC. Psammoma bodies are also seen in serous papillary ovarian carcinoma and meningioma. Schaumann and asteroid bodies are seen in sarcoidosis.
highYield: Psammoma bodies + Orphan Annie nuclei + nuclear grooves = pathognomonic triad of papillary thyroid carcinoma
pyqNote: Psammoma bodies in PTC have been directly asked in NEET PG/MCI exams multiple times. Always associate with PTC.

subject: Surgery
stem: Papillary thyroid carcinoma is the most common thyroid malignancy. Which of the following molecular alterations is most commonly associated with sporadic papillary thyroid carcinoma?
options: RAS point mutation
BRAF V600E mutation
RET germline mutation
PAX8-PPARgamma rearrangement
explanation: BRAF V600E mutation is the most common molecular alteration in sporadic papillary thyroid carcinoma, found in approximately 45-60% of cases. It results in constitutive activation of the MAPK signaling pathway. RET/PTC rearrangements are also seen in PTC but are more common in radiation-induced PTC. RET germline mutations are associated with medullary thyroid carcinoma (MEN2). PAX8-PPARgamma rearrangement is characteristic of follicular thyroid carcinoma. RAS mutations are seen in follicular thyroid carcinoma and the follicular variant of PTC.
highYield: BRAF V600E = most common mutation in sporadic PTC; RET/PTC rearrangement = radiation-induced PTC; RET germline = MTC
pyqNote: Molecular markers of thyroid cancer are high-yield. BRAF V600E for PTC, RET for MTC are frequently tested.

subject: Surgery
stem: A 35-year-old woman undergoes surgery for a 0.8 cm unifocal papillary thyroid carcinoma with no extrathyroidal extension, no lymph node involvement, and no distant metastases. She has no history of radiation exposure. What is the most appropriate surgical procedure?
options: Total thyroidectomy with central neck dissection
Total thyroidectomy alone
Thyroid lobectomy (hemithyroidectomy)
Near-total thyroidectomy with RAI ablation
explanation: For low-risk papillary thyroid carcinoma (tumor <1 cm, unifocal, no extrathyroidal extension, no LN involvement, no distant metastasis, no history of radiation), thyroid lobectomy (hemithyroidectomy) is adequate and accepted as definitive treatment per ATA 2015 guidelines. Total thyroidectomy is required for tumors >4 cm, bilateral disease, extrathyroidal extension, known nodal or distant metastases, or high-risk features. This patient's tumor is unifocal, <1 cm (microcarcinoma), low-risk - lobectomy is appropriate and avoids the risks of bilateral surgery (bilateral RLN injury, permanent hypoparathyroidism).
highYield: PTC <1 cm + unifocal + low risk = lobectomy is sufficient. Total thyroidectomy for all other PTC cases.
pyqNote: ATA guidelines on extent of thyroid surgery for PTC based on tumor size and risk are frequently tested in NEET PG.

subject: Surgery
stem: A 40-year-old woman is diagnosed with papillary thyroid carcinoma and undergoes total thyroidectomy. She is classified as high-risk (T3N1M0). Regarding her postoperative management, which of the following is the correct sequence?
options: Start L-thyroxine immediately, then radioiodine I-131 in 6 weeks
Radioiodine I-131 ablation first, then TSH suppression with L-thyroxine
Wait 6 months before any adjuvant therapy
Only TSH suppression; radioiodine is contraindicated after total thyroidectomy
explanation: For high-risk differentiated thyroid cancer (papillary or follicular) after total thyroidectomy, the management is: (1) Allow TSH to rise (stop thyroxine or use recombinant TSH) to stimulate I-131 uptake, (2) Radioiodine I-131 ablation to destroy remnant thyroid tissue and metastases, (3) Then start TSH suppression with L-thyroxine to keep TSH below 0.1 mU/L. The sequence is RAI ablation first (requiring hypothyroid state or rhTSH stimulation), then lifelong L-thyroxine suppression. This helps detect recurrence on follow-up thyroglobulin levels and whole-body scans.
highYield: High-risk PTC/FTC: Total thyroidectomy → RAI I-131 ablation → TSH suppression with L-thyroxine. RAI requires residual TSH elevation.
pyqNote: Sequence of management in differentiated thyroid cancer post-thyroidectomy is a classic NEET PG question.

subject: Surgery
stem: A 45-year-old man undergoes total thyroidectomy for papillary thyroid carcinoma with lateral neck lymph node involvement. On postoperative day 1, he develops perioral tingling, positive Chvostek sign, and serum calcium of 7.2 mg/dL. What is the most likely cause and immediate management?
options: Recurrent laryngeal nerve injury; treat with voice rest
Hypoparathyroidism due to parathyroid devascularization; give IV calcium gluconate
Thyroid storm; give propranolol and Lugol's iodine
Tracheomalacia; emergent tracheostomy
explanation: Post-thyroidectomy hypocalcemia is the most common complication after total thyroidectomy, occurring due to inadvertent removal or devascularization of parathyroid glands. Symptoms include perioral tingling, paresthesias, carpopedal spasm, Chvostek sign (facial muscle twitch with tapping over facial nerve), and Trousseau sign (carpal spasm with BP cuff inflation). Serum calcium <8 mg/dL confirms hypocalcemia. Immediate management is IV calcium gluconate (10 mL of 10% solution over 10 minutes), followed by oral calcium and vitamin D supplementation. Bilateral RLN injury causes stridor/respiratory distress, not hypocalcemia.
highYield: Post-thyroidectomy hypocalcemia: Chvostek + Trousseau signs, perioral tingling. Treat with IV calcium gluconate acutely, then oral Ca + Vit D.
pyqNote: Complications of thyroidectomy (hypocalcemia, RLN injury) are perennial NEET PG favorites. Know signs and immediate management.

subject: Surgery
stem: A 55-year-old woman presents with a solitary thyroid nodule. FNAC shows follicular cells arranged in a microfollicular pattern. She undergoes thyroid lobectomy, and histopathology reveals capsular invasion and vascular invasion. What is the diagnosis and what distinguishes this from follicular adenoma?
options: Papillary thyroid carcinoma; distinguished by Orphan Annie nuclei
Follicular thyroid carcinoma; distinguished by presence of capsular and/or vascular invasion
Medullary thyroid carcinoma; distinguished by calcitonin staining
Hurthle cell adenoma; distinguished by oxyphilic cells
explanation: Follicular thyroid carcinoma (FTC) cannot be distinguished from follicular adenoma on FNAC - both show follicular cells and the cytology is reported as 'indeterminate' (Bethesda Category IV or V). The distinction is made ONLY on histological examination of the surgical specimen by demonstrating capsular invasion (tumor cells penetrating the entire capsule) and/or vascular invasion. FNAC showing follicular cells mandates surgical excision (lobectomy) for definitive diagnosis. FTC spreads hematogenously to bones and lungs (unlike PTC which spreads via lymphatics).
highYield: FTC vs follicular adenoma: ONLY distinguished by capsular/vascular invasion on histology, NOT on FNAC. FNAC = indeterminate → surgery needed.
pyqNote: The limitation of FNAC in follicular lesions and hematogenous spread of FTC are classic NEET PG exam points.

subject: Surgery
stem: A 60-year-old woman with follicular thyroid carcinoma presents with a pathological fracture of the femur. X-ray shows a lytic expansile lesion with a 'blow-out' appearance. Which of the following best explains the spread pattern of this tumor?
options: Lymphatic spread to regional lymph nodes
Hematogenous spread to bone and lung
Direct extension to surrounding soft tissue
Transcoelomic spread through body cavities
explanation: Follicular thyroid carcinoma characteristically spreads via the hematogenous route, most commonly to bones (producing lytic 'blow-out' lesions, especially in skull, spine, pelvis, and femur) and lungs. This is in contrast to papillary thyroid carcinoma, which spreads via lymphatics to central and lateral neck lymph nodes. The hematogenous spread of FTC explains why patients may present with bone pain or pathological fractures as the first manifestation. The 'blow-out' lytic bone metastasis from thyroid carcinoma is a classic radiological finding.
highYield: FTC = hematogenous spread → bone (lytic blow-out lesions) and lung. PTC = lymphatic spread → central + lateral neck LN.
pyqNote: Spread pattern differences between FTC (hematogenous) and PTC (lymphatic) are directly tested in NEET PG.

subject: Surgery
topic: Thyroid Cancer - Hurthle Cell & Special Variants
stem: A 65-year-old woman undergoes total thyroidectomy for a thyroid tumor. Histology shows a tumor composed predominantly of large polygonal cells with abundant granular eosinophilic cytoplasm (oxyphilic cells/Askanazy cells) with capsular and vascular invasion. Post-operatively, she is planned for radioiodine therapy but the nuclear medicine physician notes poor iodine uptake. Which thyroid cancer variant does this represent?
options: Papillary thyroid carcinoma, tall cell variant
Hurthle cell carcinoma (oncocytic carcinoma)
Medullary thyroid carcinoma
Anaplastic thyroid carcinoma
explanation: Hurthle cell carcinoma (oncocytic carcinoma) is an aggressive variant of follicular thyroid carcinoma composed of oxyphilic (Hurthle/Askanazy) cells - large polygonal cells with abundant granular eosinophilic cytoplasm due to mitochondria-packed cytoplasm. It is distinguished from Hurthle cell adenoma by capsular and vascular invasion. A key characteristic is that it poorly concentrates radioiodine (I-131) compared to regular FTC, making RAI therapy less effective. It has a worse prognosis than regular FTC with higher rates of lymph node metastasis and distant spread.
highYield: Hurthle cell carcinoma: oxyphilic cells + capsular/vascular invasion + POOR radioiodine uptake + aggressive behavior.
pyqNote: Hurthle cell carcinoma's poor RAI uptake distinguishing it from regular FTC is a high-yield surgical oncology point.

subject: Surgery
stem: A 35-year-old man presents with a thyroid nodule. His brother recently died of thyroid cancer. FNAC shows sheets of polygonal and spindle cells with amyloid deposits in the stroma. Immunohistochemistry is positive for calcitonin. Which cells give rise to this tumor?
options: Follicular cells (thyrocytes)
Parafollicular C-cells
Hurthle cells (oxyphilic cells)
Lymphocytes infiltrating the thyroid
explanation: Medullary thyroid carcinoma (MTC) arises from parafollicular C-cells (clear cells) of the thyroid gland, which are neural crest-derived cells that produce calcitonin. The tumor cells are positive for calcitonin on immunohistochemistry, and serum calcitonin is used as a tumor marker for diagnosis, screening family members, and monitoring for recurrence. Amyloid deposits in the stroma (derived from procalcitonin) are a characteristic histological feature. Unlike differentiated thyroid cancers (which arise from follicular cells), MTC does not take up radioiodine.
highYield: MTC: C-cells → calcitonin marker + amyloid stroma + IHC calcitonin positive + NO radioiodine uptake.
pyqNote: Cell of origin and tumor marker of MTC (C-cells, calcitonin) are basic but frequently tested NEET PG questions.

subject: Surgery
stem: A 30-year-old woman is diagnosed with medullary thyroid carcinoma. Genetic testing reveals a germline RET proto-oncogene mutation (C634F). Her 8-year-old son has no symptoms. What is the most appropriate next step for the son?
options: Annual ultrasound thyroid starting at age 10
Serum calcitonin stimulation test with pentagastrin or calcium
RET genetic testing; if positive, prophylactic total thyroidectomy
No action needed until symptoms develop
explanation: Hereditary MTC (25% of all MTC cases) is associated with germline RET proto-oncogene mutations and is autosomal dominant. In MEN2A, prophylactic thyroidectomy timing is based on the specific RET codon mutation (risk category): high-risk codons (e.g., C634) - thyroidectomy by age 5 years. For a child of an affected parent, RET genetic testing should be performed. If positive, prophylactic total thyroidectomy is performed at an age dictated by the mutation risk level. Calcitonin stimulation testing (pentagastrin or calcium infusion) was previously used before genetic testing was available and is now less commonly used for screening.
highYield: Hereditary MTC: RET germline mutation → screen family with genetic testing → prophylactic thyroidectomy based on mutation risk category.
pyqNote: Prophylactic thyroidectomy for RET mutation carriers in MEN2 is a high-yield clinical scenario in NEET PG surgery.

subject: Surgery
stem: A patient is diagnosed with MEN2B syndrome. Which of the following combination of features would you expect?
options: Medullary thyroid carcinoma + primary hyperparathyroidism + pheochromocytoma
Medullary thyroid carcinoma + pheochromocytoma + mucosal neuromas + marfanoid habitus
Medullary thyroid carcinoma + pheochromocytoma + parathyroid hyperplasia + cafe-au-lait spots
Pituitary adenoma + pancreatic tumors + parathyroid adenoma
explanation: MEN2B (Multiple Endocrine Neoplasia type 2B) is characterized by: (1) Medullary thyroid carcinoma (MTC) - most aggressive, develops earliest, (2) Pheochromocytoma (50%), (3) Mucosal neuromas (lips, tongue, eyelids), (4) Marfanoid habitus (tall, long limbs), and (5) Intestinal ganglioneuromatosis. Unlike MEN2A, there is NO hyperparathyroidism in MEN2B. MEN2A includes MTC + pheochromocytoma + primary hyperparathyroidism. MEN1 (Wermer syndrome) includes pituitary + pancreatic + parathyroid tumors. MEN2B is associated with RET codon 918 mutation (highest risk) and MTC appears in infancy.
highYield: MEN2B: MTC + Pheo + Mucosal neuromas + Marfanoid habitus (NO hyperparathyroidism). MEN2A: MTC + Pheo + Hyperparathyroidism.
pyqNote: Distinguishing MEN2A from MEN2B features is a classic NEET PG multiple-choice trap question.

subject: Surgery
stem: A 75-year-old woman presents with a rapidly enlarging neck mass of 6 weeks duration causing dysphagia and stridor. She has had a goitre for 20 years. CT scan shows a large heterogeneous thyroid mass with tracheal compression, lymph node enlargement, and lung nodules. Biopsy shows bizarre giant cells and spindle cells with numerous mitoses. What is the most likely diagnosis and prognosis?
options: Papillary thyroid carcinoma; excellent prognosis with total thyroidectomy
Follicular thyroid carcinoma; good prognosis with radioiodine
Anaplastic (undifferentiated) thyroid carcinoma; median survival <6 months
Lymphoma of thyroid; curable with chemotherapy
explanation: Anaplastic thyroid carcinoma (ATC) is the most aggressive thyroid malignancy with the worst prognosis of all thyroid cancers. It typically presents in elderly patients (>65 years) with a pre-existing goitre. Features include: rapid growth, early invasion of adjacent structures (trachea, esophagus), bizarre pleomorphic cells with spindle and giant cell morphology, high mitotic rate, and early distant metastases. It does not take up radioiodine (dedifferentiated). Median survival is 3-6 months; 1-year survival <20%. Treatment is palliative (external beam RT ± chemotherapy with doxorubicin). It is classified as Stage IV at diagnosis by AJCC.
highYield: ATC: Elderly + rapidly enlarging goitre + worst prognosis (median survival <6 months) + Stage IV at diagnosis + no RAI uptake + palliative treatment.
pyqNote: ATC as the thyroid cancer with worst prognosis in elderly patients is a frequently tested NEET PG fact.

subject: Surgery
stem: During total thyroidectomy, a 40-year-old woman's right recurrent laryngeal nerve is inadvertently divided. She recovers from surgery but develops hoarseness. Two months later, if the left RLN is also injured during a re-exploration, what would be the most dangerous complication?
options: Permanent hoarseness and change in voice quality
Bilateral vocal cord paralysis causing respiratory obstruction requiring tracheostomy
Hypocalcemia due to parathyroid devascularization
Dysphagia due to pharyngeal dysfunction
explanation: Unilateral RLN injury results in ipsilateral vocal cord paralysis causing hoarseness (the contralateral cord compensates). Bilateral RLN injury causes both vocal cords to assume the paramedian position (adducted), leading to severe respiratory distress and stridor requiring emergency tracheostomy. This is the most dangerous complication of bilateral RLN injury. The RLN supplies all intrinsic laryngeal muscles except the cricothyroid (supplied by superior laryngeal nerve). The right RLN hooks around the subclavian artery; the left hooks around the arch of aorta at the ligamentum arteriosum - making it longer and more vulnerable.
highYield: Bilateral RLN injury → both cords in paramedian position → acute airway obstruction → emergency tracheostomy. Unilateral = hoarseness only.
pyqNote: Consequences of unilateral vs bilateral RLN injury and the need for tracheostomy are high-yield surgical anatomy questions.

subject: Surgery
stem: A 45-year-old woman undergoes screening mammography. The radiologist reports a 2 cm spiculated mass with microcalcifications and skin thickening, classified as BIRADS 5. What does this classification indicate and what is the recommended next step?
options: Probably benign; repeat mammogram in 6 months
Benign finding; routine annual screening
Highly suspicious for malignancy; tissue biopsy is required
Known malignancy; correlate with prior biopsy results
explanation: The BIRADS (Breast Imaging Reporting and Data System) classification: BIRADS 0 = incomplete, BIRADS 1 = negative (routine screening), BIRADS 2 = benign (routine screening), BIRADS 3 = probably benign (<2% malignancy risk, 6-month follow-up), BIRADS 4 = suspicious (2-95% risk, biopsy recommended), BIRADS 5 = highly suspicious (>95% malignancy risk, biopsy required), BIRADS 6 = known malignancy (biopsy-proven, awaiting treatment). BIRADS 5 indicates a highly suspicious lesion requiring tissue biopsy (core needle biopsy preferred for histology and receptor status). Spiculated mass with microcalcifications is a classic malignant mammographic appearance.
highYield: BIRADS 5 = highly suspicious (>95% malignancy) → tissue biopsy mandatory. BIRADS 3 = probably benign → 6-month follow-up.
pyqNote: BIRADS classification and management at each level are standard NEET PG surgical oncology questions.

subject: Surgery
topic: Breast Cancer - FNAC and Core Biopsy
stem: A 50-year-old woman has a palpable breast lump classified as BIRADS 4 on mammography. FNAC is performed and reported as C4 (suspicious). The next step is core needle biopsy. What additional information does core needle biopsy provide that FNAC cannot?
options: Better visualization of microcalcifications
Determination of tumor size on imaging
Histological architecture, ER/PR/HER2 receptor status, and Nottingham grade
Lymph node status assessment
explanation: FNAC (C1-C5 classification): C1=inadequate, C2=benign, C3=atypia/probably benign, C4=suspicious, C5=malignant. FNAC provides cytology only - it can suggest malignancy but cannot determine: (1) histological architecture (invasive vs in-situ), (2) ER/PR/HER2 receptor status, (3) Nottingham histological grade (tubule formation + nuclear pleomorphism + mitotic count), (4) lymphovascular invasion. Core needle biopsy (Tru-Cut) provides histology with tissue architecture, enabling definitive cancer typing, receptor status (essential for treatment planning - tamoxifen for ER+, trastuzumab for HER2+), and grading. All invasive breast cancers must have ER/PR/HER2 testing on core biopsy.
highYield: Core needle biopsy > FNAC: provides histology, ER/PR/HER2 status, Nottingham grade - all essential for treatment planning.
pyqNote: Advantages of core biopsy over FNAC for receptor testing in breast cancer is a modern high-yield NEET PG topic.

subject: Surgery
stem: A 48-year-old woman has a 2.5 cm, Grade 2, ER+/PR+/HER2- invasive ductal carcinoma in the upper outer quadrant of the left breast with no palpable axillary lymph nodes. She is suitable for either mastectomy or breast conservation. Regarding surgical options, which statement is most accurate?
options: Modified radical mastectomy gives better survival than breast-conserving surgery
Wide local excision (WLE) with postoperative radiotherapy is oncologically equivalent to mastectomy
WLE alone without radiotherapy is sufficient for early-stage breast cancer
Sentinel lymph node biopsy is contraindicated in clinically node-negative patients
explanation: Multiple randomized controlled trials (NSABP B-06, Milan trials) have established that breast-conserving surgery (wide local excision/lumpectomy) with postoperative radiotherapy provides equivalent survival to mastectomy for early-stage breast cancer (Stage I-II). The key is achieving clear margins. WLE alone without RT is NOT adequate - radiotherapy reduces local recurrence from ~30% to <10%. Therefore, the combination of WLE + RT is the standard of care for early breast cancer when feasible. Sentinel lymph node biopsy (SLNB) is the standard of care for axillary staging in clinically node-negative patients, avoiding routine axillary clearance.
highYield: WLE + RT = mastectomy in terms of survival for early breast cancer. RT is mandatory with breast conservation. SLNB for node-negative patients.
pyqNote: Equivalence of breast conservation + RT vs mastectomy is a landmark concept repeatedly tested in NEET PG.

subject: Surgery
stem: A 55-year-old woman undergoes wide local excision and sentinel lymph node biopsy for a T2 invasive breast cancer. The sentinel lymph node (SLN) shows micrometastasis (0.2-2 mm). She is planned for whole-breast radiotherapy. According to ACOSOG Z0011 trial criteria, what is the appropriate management of the axilla?
options: Axillary lymph node dissection (ALND) of all levels
No further axillary surgery; complete planned whole-breast RT
Axillary radiation therapy as alternative to ALND
Repeat sentinel node biopsy to confirm micrometastasis
explanation: The ACOSOG Z0011 trial established that in patients with T1-2 breast cancer undergoing breast-conserving surgery with whole-breast radiotherapy who have 1-2 positive SLNs, completion ALND does NOT improve overall survival or disease-free survival compared to observation. Therefore, for patients meeting Z0011 criteria (1-2 positive SLN, BCS + whole-breast RT planned, clinically node-negative preoperatively), ALND can be omitted. This avoids lymphedema and arm morbidity. ALND remains indicated if: >2 positive SLN, mastectomy without RT, matted nodes, or gross extracapsular extension.
highYield: Z0011 trial: 1-2 positive SLN + BCS + whole-breast RT → no ALND needed. ALND if >2 positive nodes or mastectomy without RT.
pyqNote: ACOSOG Z0011 trial and its implications for axillary management are increasingly tested in NEET PG surgical oncology.

subject: Surgery
stem: A 42-year-old premenopausal woman undergoes surgery for ER+/PR+/HER2- Stage II breast cancer. She receives adjuvant chemotherapy (AC-T regimen). What endocrine therapy should she receive, and for how long?
options: Anastrozole (aromatase inhibitor) for 5 years
Tamoxifen for 5-10 years
Tamoxifen for 2 years then aromatase inhibitor for 3 years
No endocrine therapy needed since she received chemotherapy
explanation: For premenopausal women with hormone receptor-positive (ER+/PR+) breast cancer, the standard endocrine therapy is tamoxifen (a selective estrogen receptor modulator, SERM). Current ASCO guidelines recommend 5-10 years of tamoxifen for premenopausal women (extended adjuvant therapy for 10 years reduces recurrence further per ATLAS and aTTom trials). Aromatase inhibitors (anastrozole, letrozole, exemestane) are NOT effective in premenopausal women unless combined with ovarian suppression, because they only block peripheral estrogen synthesis but not ovarian estrogen production. Tamoxifen remains the standard for premenopausal ER+ breast cancer.
highYield: Premenopausal ER+ breast cancer → Tamoxifen 5-10 years. Postmenopausal ER+ → Aromatase inhibitor 5-10 years. Key distinction.
pyqNote: Tamoxifen for premenopausal vs aromatase inhibitor for postmenopausal ER+ breast cancer is a classic NEET PG differentiator.

subject: Surgery
stem: A 50-year-old postmenopausal woman has HER2-positive (IHC 3+), ER-negative breast cancer. After surgery and chemotherapy, which targeted agent should be included in her adjuvant regimen?
options: Tamoxifen for 5 years
Trastuzumab (Herceptin) for 1 year
CDK4/6 inhibitor (palbociclib) for 2 years
Bevacizumab (anti-VEGF) for 6 months
explanation: Trastuzumab (Herceptin) is a monoclonal antibody targeting HER2 (human epidermal growth factor receptor 2). It is indicated for HER2-positive breast cancer (IHC 3+ or FISH amplified). Adjuvant trastuzumab for 1 year significantly improves disease-free survival and overall survival in HER2+ early breast cancer (HERA trial, NSABP B-31). Key side effect: cardiotoxicity (cardiomyopathy) - requires baseline ECHO/MUGA scan and monitoring. CDK4/6 inhibitors (palbociclib, ribociclib, abemaciclib) are used for HR+/HER2- metastatic breast cancer. Tamoxifen/AI are for ER+ disease. Bevacizumab is not a standard adjuvant breast cancer treatment.
highYield: HER2+ breast cancer → Trastuzumab for 1 year. Monitor cardiac function (LVEF). HER2 tested by IHC/FISH on all invasive tumors.
pyqNote: Trastuzumab for HER2+ breast cancer and its cardiotoxicity monitoring are high-yield NEET PG pharmacology-surgery interface questions.

subject: Surgery
stem: A 52-year-old woman presents with a T4b (skin involvement) N2 (fixed axillary nodes) breast cancer (locally advanced). She is ER-/PR-/HER2- (triple negative). What is the most appropriate initial management?
options: Immediate modified radical mastectomy followed by chemotherapy
Neoadjuvant chemotherapy first, then surgery, then radiotherapy
Palliative radiotherapy only
Hormonal therapy with tamoxifen
explanation: Locally advanced breast cancer (LABC) - T3/T4 or N2/N3 - is managed with neoadjuvant (preoperative) chemotherapy first, typically with an anthracycline-taxane regimen (AC-T). Benefits of neoadjuvant chemotherapy: (1) Downstages the tumor enabling surgery (sometimes converting inoperable to operable or enabling breast conservation), (2) Tests in-vivo chemo sensitivity, (3) Pathological complete response (pCR) is a surrogate endpoint for prognosis, (4) For triple-negative breast cancer, pCR correlates with excellent survival. After neoadjuvant chemo, surgery (mastectomy or BCS if downstaged), then adjuvant RT. This patient is inoperable initially due to T4b disease.
highYield: LABC (T3/T4, N2/N3): Neoadjuvant chemotherapy first → surgery → adjuvant RT. Never primary surgery for LABC.
pyqNote: Neoadjuvant chemotherapy as the initial treatment for locally advanced breast cancer is a frequently tested clinical scenario in NEET PG.

subject: Surgery
stem: Pheochromocytoma is described by the 'Rule of 10s'. Which of the following correctly represents these features?
options: 10% malignant, 10% bilateral, 10% extra-adrenal, 10% familial, 10% calcified
10% occur in children, 10% are associated with MEN1, 10% cause hypertension, 10% are benign
10% are right-sided, 10% secrete cortisol, 10% occur in females, 10% cause Cushing syndrome
10% are located in the organ of Zuckerkandl, 10% present with weight gain, 10% are ACTH-secreting
explanation: The classic 'Rule of 10s' for pheochromocytoma: 10% are malignant, 10% are bilateral (25-50% in hereditary cases), 10% are extra-adrenal (paragangliomas, most commonly at the organ of Zuckerkandl at aortic bifurcation), 10% are familial (associated with MEN2A, MEN2B, VHL syndrome, NF1, SDH mutations), and 10% are calcified. Extra-adrenal pheochromocytomas are more commonly malignant. Bilateral pheochromocytomas should raise suspicion for hereditary syndromes (MEN2, VHL). Malignancy is defined by metastasis (local invasion alone is not sufficient for diagnosis of malignancy).
highYield: Rule of 10s: 10% bilateral, 10% extra-adrenal, 10% malignant, 10% familial, 10% calcified. Extra-adrenal = more often malignant.
pyqNote: The Rule of 10s for pheochromocytoma is one of the most commonly tested facts in NEET PG surgery and medicine.

subject: Surgery
stem: A 38-year-old man with hypertension, episodic headaches, sweating, and palpitations has elevated urine metanephrines. CT scan shows a 4 cm right adrenal mass. He is scheduled for laparoscopic adrenalectomy in 2 weeks. What is the correct sequence of preoperative pharmacological preparation?
options: Start beta-blocker (propranolol) first for 10-14 days, then add alpha-blocker (phenoxybenzamine)
Start alpha-blocker (phenoxybenzamine) first for 10-14 days, then add beta-blocker if needed for tachycardia
Start both alpha and beta blockers simultaneously on day 1
No preoperative medication needed; control BP intraoperatively with sodium nitroprusside
explanation: The correct preoperative preparation for pheochromocytoma is ALPHA-BLOCKADE FIRST with phenoxybenzamine (non-selective, irreversible alpha-blocker) for 10-14 days before surgery, THEN add beta-blocker (propranolol) if tachycardia occurs. The critical reason: if beta-blockers are given FIRST (or alone), they block beta-2 mediated vasodilation, leaving alpha-1 mediated vasoconstriction unopposed, causing a hypertensive crisis. Only after adequate alpha-blockade can a beta-blocker be safely added. Phenoxybenzamine causes reflex tachycardia (due to decreased BP), requiring beta-blockade after alpha-blockade is established. Doxazosin (selective alpha-1 blocker) is an alternative. High-salt diet and increased fluid intake are also recommended to correct volume depletion.
highYield: PHEO pre-op: Alpha-block FIRST (phenoxybenzamine 10-14 days) → THEN beta-block if tachycardia. Never beta-first = hypertensive crisis (unopposed alpha).
pyqNote: Alpha before beta blockade in pheochromocytoma is one of the most tested pharmacological principles in NEET PG surgery.

subject: Surgery
stem: A 25-year-old woman presents with bilateral pheochromocytoma. She also has hemangioblastomas of the cerebellum and retinal angiomas. Genetic testing reveals a germline mutation. Which syndrome does this represent and which gene is mutated?
options: MEN2A; RET proto-oncogene mutation
Von Hippel-Lindau (VHL) disease; VHL tumor suppressor gene mutation
Neurofibromatosis type 1 (NF1); NF1 tumor suppressor gene mutation
Succinate dehydrogenase (SDH) mutation syndrome; SDHB gene mutation
explanation: Von Hippel-Lindau (VHL) disease is an autosomal dominant condition caused by germline mutations in the VHL tumor suppressor gene (chromosome 3p25). Features include: hemangioblastomas (cerebellum, brainstem, spinal cord, retina), renal cell carcinoma (clear cell type), pheochromocytoma (often bilateral), pancreatic cysts and neuroendocrine tumors, and endolymphatic sac tumors. MEN2A (RET mutation) has MTC + pheo + hyperparathyroidism. NF1 has cafe-au-lait spots, neurofibromas, and pheo. SDH mutations (SDHB especially) are associated with extra-adrenal paragangliomas and higher malignancy risk. The combination of cerebellar hemangioblastoma + retinal angiomas + bilateral pheo = VHL.
highYield: VHL disease: VHL gene mutation → hemangioblastoma (cerebellum/retina) + bilateral pheo + RCC + pancreatic lesions.
pyqNote: VHL syndrome associations and distinguishing it from MEN2A and SDH mutations is a high-yield NEET PG genetics-surgery question.

subject: Surgery
stem: A 45-year-old hypertensive woman presents with paroxysmal episodes of headache, sweating, and palpitations. Blood pressure during an episode is 220/130 mmHg. Which investigation has the highest sensitivity for diagnosing pheochromocytoma?
options: 24-hour urinary vanillylmandelic acid (VMA)
24-hour urinary catecholamines (epinephrine + norepinephrine)
Plasma free metanephrines (normetanephrine + metanephrine)
CT abdomen with contrast
explanation: Plasma free metanephrines (normetanephrine and metanephrine) have the highest sensitivity (>95%) for detecting pheochromocytoma and are now the recommended first-line biochemical test. Metanephrines are the stable O-methylated metabolites of catecholamines, continuously produced by the tumor independent of episodic release, making them more reliable than episodic catecholamine measurements. 24-hour urine metanephrines also have high sensitivity (~97%). 24-hour urine VMA has lower sensitivity (~65%) and more false positives. Urinary catecholamines have moderate sensitivity. CT/MRI are for tumor localization AFTER biochemical confirmation. MIBG scintigraphy is used for extra-adrenal/metastatic disease.
highYield: Plasma free metanephrines = highest sensitivity for pheo diagnosis (>95%). Biochemical confirmation before imaging. VMA = low sensitivity (historical).
pyqNote: Biochemical diagnosis of pheochromocytoma and the superiority of plasma metanephrines are high-yield NEET PG investigation questions.

subject: Surgery
stem: A 50-year-old man with a large multinodular goitre is scheduled for total thyroidectomy. He denies symptoms of hyperthyroidism. Preoperative TFTs show TSH 0.02 mIU/L, free T4 elevated, free T3 elevated. What is the most appropriate preoperative management?
options: Proceed directly to surgery; thyroid function is irrelevant for surgical planning
Render euthyroid with antithyroid drugs (carbimazole/propylthiouracil) before surgery
Start propranolol only and proceed to surgery within 48 hours
Cancel surgery; hyperthyroid patients can never undergo thyroidectomy
explanation: All patients undergoing thyroid surgery must be rendered euthyroid (normal thyroid function) before surgery to prevent thyroid storm (thyrotoxic crisis), which can be precipitated by anesthesia, surgical manipulation of the thyroid, or perioperative stress. Antithyroid drugs (carbimazole or propylthiouracil) are used for 6-8 weeks. Potassium iodide (Lugol's iodine) for 10 days before surgery reduces vascularity of the gland and decreases intraoperative bleeding. Beta-blockers (propranolol) control symptoms. Thyroid storm (fever >40°C, severe tachycardia, agitation, hypotension) is a life-threatening complication prevented by achieving euthyroid state preoperatively.
highYield: Preop thyroid surgery: MUST be euthyroid. Give antithyroid drugs → euthyroid state → surgery. Lugol's iodine 10 days before to reduce vascularity.
pyqNote: Preoperative euthyroid requirement and thyroid storm prevention are classic NEET PG anesthesia-surgery interface questions.

subject: Surgery
stem: A 60-year-old postmenopausal woman with HR+/HER2- metastatic breast cancer develops progression on letrozole monotherapy. Which class of targeted agents combined with an aromatase inhibitor has shown significant improvement in progression-free survival in this setting?
options: mTOR inhibitors (everolimus)
CDK4/6 inhibitors (palbociclib, ribociclib, abemaciclib)
PARP inhibitors (olaparib)
Checkpoint inhibitors (pembrolizumab)
explanation: CDK4/6 inhibitors (palbociclib, ribociclib, abemaciclib) in combination with endocrine therapy (letrozole or fulvestrant) have revolutionized treatment of HR+/HER2- metastatic breast cancer. Multiple trials (PALOMA-2, MONALEESA-2, MONARCH-3) demonstrated doubling of progression-free survival from ~14-15 months to ~24-27 months. CDK4/6 inhibitors block the cell cycle at G1-S transition by inhibiting CDK4/6-cyclin D1 complex, preventing RB phosphorylation and E2F transcription factor release. Key side effects: neutropenia, fatigue, nausea. mTOR inhibitors (everolimus + exemestane: BOLERO-2) are another option. PARP inhibitors are for BRCA1/2-mutated HER2- metastatic breast cancer.
highYield: HR+/HER2- metastatic breast cancer: CDK4/6 inhibitor (palbociclib/ribociclib/abemaciclib) + AI = standard of care. Doubles PFS.
pyqNote: CDK4/6 inhibitors in HR+/HER2- metastatic breast cancer represent the latest high-yield pharmacology additions to NEET PG oncology.

subject: Surgery
stem: A 35-year-old man undergoes total thyroidectomy for a 3 cm papillary thyroid carcinoma. Intraoperative frozen section of a central neck (level VI) lymph node is positive. What is the standard recommendation regarding lymph node dissection?
options: Lateral neck dissection (levels II-V) only, preserving central neck nodes
Central neck dissection (level VI) at time of thyroidectomy for node-positive central neck
No lymph node dissection; treat with radioiodine alone
Radical neck dissection including all levels I-VI and sternocleidomastoid muscle
explanation: PTC spreads via lymphatics, first to central neck (level VI - pretracheal, paratracheal nodes) and then to lateral neck (levels II-V). ATA 2015 guidelines recommend therapeutic central neck dissection (level VI) when central neck LN involvement is documented preoperatively or intraoperatively. Prophylactic central neck dissection may be considered for T3-T4 tumors even without clinical nodal involvement. Lateral neck dissection (levels II-V, compartment-oriented modified radical neck dissection) is performed for documented lateral neck nodal metastasis. Classical radical neck dissection (sacrificing IJV, SCM, spinal accessory nerve) is no longer standard - modified functional neck dissection preserves these structures.
highYield: PTC nodal spread: central neck (level VI) first → lateral neck (levels II-V). Therapeutic CND for documented central neck involvement at thyroidectomy.
pyqNote: Central vs lateral neck dissection patterns in PTC and indications for prophylactic vs therapeutic dissection are increasingly tested in NEET PG.

subject: Surgery
stem: A 40-year-old woman with biochemically confirmed unilateral adrenal pheochromocytoma (4 cm, right sided) has undergone adequate preoperative alpha-blockade for 14 days. She is now prepared for surgery. What is the preferred surgical approach?
options: Open adrenalectomy via midline laparotomy
Laparoscopic adrenalectomy (transperitoneal or retroperitoneal)
Percutaneous radiofrequency ablation
Medical management only with phenoxybenzamine lifelong
explanation: Laparoscopic adrenalectomy is now the gold standard surgical approach for pheochromocytoma, provided tumors are <6-8 cm and not locally invasive. Advantages include: less blood loss, shorter hospital stay, faster recovery, less postoperative pain, and equivalent oncological outcomes to open surgery. The retroperitoneoscopic approach may be preferred for smaller tumors as it avoids intraperitoneal organ manipulation. Open surgery is reserved for: tumors >6-8 cm, suspected malignancy with local invasion, previous abdominal surgery making laparoscopy difficult, or failure of laparoscopic approach. Intraoperative hypertensive crises are managed with sodium nitroprusside or phentolamine; hypotension after tumor removal is managed with IV fluids and vasopressors (beta-blockade effect on BP).
highYield: Pheochromocytoma surgery: laparoscopic adrenalectomy (gold standard) after 10-14 days alpha-blockade. Open for >6-8 cm or invasive tumors.
pyqNote: Laparoscopic adrenalectomy as the preferred approach for pheochromocytoma and the role of preoperative alpha-blockade are standard NEET PG questions.

subject: Surgery
stem: A pathology report for invasive ductal carcinoma of the breast describes: tubule formation score 3 (poorly formed), nuclear pleomorphism score 3 (marked), mitotic count score 2 (moderate). What is the Nottingham histological grade?
options: Grade 1 (well differentiated, score 3-5)
Grade 2 (moderately differentiated, score 6-7)
Grade 3 (poorly differentiated, score 8-9)
Grade cannot be determined from this information
explanation: The Nottingham grading system (Elston-Ellis modification of Scarff-Bloom-Richardson) grades invasive breast carcinoma based on three components, each scored 1-3: (1) Tubule formation (1=majority, 2=moderate, 3=minimal), (2) Nuclear pleomorphism (1=small uniform, 2=moderate, 3=marked), (3) Mitotic count per 10 HPF (scored based on absolute counts). Total score: 3-5 = Grade 1 (well differentiated, best prognosis), 6-7 = Grade 2 (moderately differentiated), 8-9 = Grade 3 (poorly differentiated, worst prognosis). This patient: 3+3+2 = 8, which is Grade 3 (poorly differentiated). Grade 3 tumors are associated with higher Ki-67, triple-negative phenotype, and worse prognosis.
highYield: Nottingham grade: Tubules + Nuclear pleomorphism + Mitosis, each 1-3. Total 3-5=Grade1, 6-7=Grade2, 8-9=Grade3.
pyqNote: Nottingham grading calculation and the three components are directly tested in NEET PG breast cancer pathology questions.

subject: Surgery
topic: Thyroid Cancer - Medullary Thyroid Carcinoma Calcitonin
stem: A 45-year-old man is found to have a thyroid nodule on ultrasound. Serum calcitonin is markedly elevated at 850 pg/mL. FNAC shows cells positive for calcitonin and CEA. After total thyroidectomy confirms medullary thyroid carcinoma, what is the most appropriate next step for his family members?
options: Annual thyroid ultrasound for all first-degree relatives
Serum calcitonin stimulation test with pentagastrin for all relatives
RET proto-oncogene germline genetic testing for all first-degree relatives
Prophylactic total thyroidectomy for all relatives regardless of test results
explanation: Approximately 25% of MTC cases are hereditary (associated with germline RET mutations: MEN2A, MEN2B, or Familial MTC). All newly diagnosed MTC patients should undergo RET proto-oncogene germline testing. If a pathogenic RET mutation is identified in the index patient, all first-degree relatives should be offered RET genetic testing. Positive family members should undergo prophylactic total thyroidectomy at an age determined by the specific codon mutation (ATA risk category: highest risk - codon 918 in MEN2B = infancy; high risk - codon 634 = age 5; moderate risk - other codons = age 5-10). Calcitonin and CEA are tumor markers used for post-treatment surveillance, not primary family screening.
highYield: MTC diagnosis → RET germline testing for patient → if positive, screen all first-degree relatives → prophylactic thyroidectomy age based on mutation risk.
pyqNote: Family screening protocol after hereditary MTC diagnosis using RET testing is a high-yield clinical management question in NEET PG.

subject: Surgery
stem: A 65-year-old man presents with a 2 cm subareolar breast lump. Core biopsy confirms invasive ductal carcinoma, ER+/PR+/HER2-. He has a family history of breast cancer. Which genetic mutation is most commonly associated with male breast cancer?
options: BRCA1 mutation
BRCA2 mutation
TP53 mutation (Li-Fraumeni syndrome)
PALB2 mutation
explanation: BRCA2 mutation is the most common hereditary predisposition for male breast cancer, accounting for approximately 10-15% of male breast cancers. Unlike female breast cancer where BRCA1 and BRCA2 contribute more equally, BRCA2 has a much stronger association with male breast cancer (lifetime risk ~6-8% with BRCA2, vs ~0.1-0.2% population risk). BRCA1 mutations can also predispose but less commonly. Male breast cancer is treated similarly to female breast cancer: modified radical mastectomy (mastectomy + SLNB/ALND), tamoxifen for ER+ disease (aromatase inhibitors less effective due to residual androgen-to-estrogen conversion). Male breast cancer represents <1% of all breast cancers.
highYield: Male breast cancer: BRCA2 mutation most common genetic risk. Treatment: mastectomy + tamoxifen for ER+. BRCA2 > BRCA1 for male breast cancer risk.
pyqNote: BRCA2 as the predominant genetic risk in male breast cancer, contrasting with female breast cancer genetics, is an emerging NEET PG topic.
